nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Mascy
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.

variants.name: Maxey
meanings: English: habitational name from a place in Northamptonshire, so named from the Old English personal name Maccus + Old English ēg ‘island’.
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Macasa ‘descendant of Macus’, a personal name of uncertain origin. It may be a variant of Magnus .

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: George
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

nameMeanings.name: Hamo
meanings: This is still found as a male baptismal name. It is derived from Hamo, who came over with the Conqueror; perhaps from the Icelandic hamr, a skin found in ulf-hamr, the nickname of a mythological king, which Cleasby translates 'wolf-skin.'

en.lifeSummary: Sir Hamo de Mascy was born in 1254. He had at least 1 son. He died in 1335, in Kelsale, Suffolk, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 81.

fr.lifeSummary: Sir Hamo de Mascy est né en 1254. Il a eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1335, à Kelsale, Suffolk, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 81 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Sir Hamo de Mascy wurde 1254 geboren. Er hatte mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1335 im Alter von 81 Jahren in Kelsale, Suffolk, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Sir Hamo de Mascy è nato il nel 1254. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio. Morì nel 1335, nel Kelsale, Suffolk, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 81 anni.
